text.title: Flexibility and Efficiency of Use Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules grounding Flexibility and Efficiency of Use. Each pairs a binary check with a sourced rationale; HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override.

rule.statement: Default path is fully usable without shortcuts; accelerators only layer on top.
rule.rationale: Forcing shortcuts on novices creates onboarding friction.

rule.statement: Shortcuts are discoverable via menus, tooltips, or a help layer; hidden shortcuts cannot accelerate users who do not find them.
Power users can personalise the workspace — saved views, custom layouts, keyboard maps — within bounded limits.
rule.rationale: Bounded personalisation respects both flexibility and consistency.

rule.statement: Repetitive flows expose batch operations and templates; per-item repetition is a smell.
rule.rationale: Repetitive flows without batch waste expert time.

rule.statement: Shortcuts honour OS conventions (cmd-s save, esc cancel); novel shortcuts require a written rationale.
rule.rationale: Custom shortcuts that fight muscle memory cause errors.

rule.statement: If preconditions in AGENTS.md "Applies If" do not all hold, OR any "Skip If" disqualifier is true, do not run this methodology — route to the named alternative.
rule.rationale: Methodologies are pre-condition gated; running them out-of-scope produces noise and burns the agent context budget.

rule.statement: If preconditions hold, execute the procedure in `content/04-procedure.xml` (or the rule set above when complexity is light), emit the artefact specified by `content/02-output-contract.xml`, and validate with `scripts/validate-flexibility-efficiency.py` before returning.
rule.rationale: Tying the entry rule to the validator script closes the loop between policy and machine check.
